content.text:
Lagrange's Theorem is one of the most fundamental results in group 
theory. It constrains the possible sizes of subgroups and has far-reaching 
applications in number theory, combinatorics, and beyond.

content.title: Lagrange's Theorem
content.content: If $G$ is a finite group and $H \leq G$, then $|H|$ divides $|G|$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The left cosets of $H$ partition $G$ (Theorem [thm:coset-partition]). 
Each coset has exactly $|H|$ elements (property (v)). If there are $k$ 
distinct cosets, then:
\[
 |G| = k \cdot |H|
\]
Thus $|H|$ divides $|G|$.
For any $g \in G$, the order $|g|$ divides $|G|$.
content.label: Corollary

content.content:
The element $g$ generates the cyclic subgroup $\langle g \rangle$ with 
$|\langle g \rangle| = |g|$. By Lagrange, $|g|$ divides $|G|$.
If $|G| = n$, then $g^n = e$ for all $g \in G$.
content.label: Corollary

content.content:
Let $|g| = d$. Then $d \mid n$, say $n = dk$. Thus $g^n = g^{dk} = 
(g^d)^k = e^k = e$.
A group of prime order $p$ is cyclic (hence isomorphic to $\Z_p$).
content.label: Corollary

content.content:
Let $|G| = p$ (prime) and let $g \in G$ with $g \neq e$. Then 
$|\langle g \rangle|$ divides $p$ and is greater than $1$ (since 
$g \neq e$). Thus $|\langle g \rangle| = p = |G|$, so $G = \langle g 
\rangle$.
A group of prime order has no proper nontrivial subgroups.
content.label: Corollary

content.title: Possible subgroup orders
content.problem:
Let $|G| = 12$. The possible subgroup orders are divisors of $12$:
\[
 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12
\]
Note: Lagrange's Theorem says only that subgroup orders must divide $|G|$. 
It does *not* guarantee that subgroups of each divisor size exist.

content.title: $A_4$ has no subgroup of order $6$
content.problem:
Although $6 \mid 12 = |A_4|$, the group $A_4$ has no subgroup of order 
$6$. The converse of Lagrange's Theorem is false!

content.text: Euler's Theorem and Fermat's Little Theorem

content.content:
The element $[a] \in U(n)$ has order dividing $|U(n)| = \phi(n)$ by 
Lagrange. Thus $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.

content.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
content.content: If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content: Since $p$ is prime, $\phi(p) = p - 1$. Apply Euler's Theorem.

items: Find all possible orders of elements in a group of order $20$.
Prove that every group of order $35$ is cyclic.
Use Fermat's Little Theorem to find $2^{100} \mod 13$.

Show that if $H, K \leq G$ with $|H| = 12$ and $|K| = 35$, then 
 $H \cap K = \{e\}$.
Prove that a group of order $4$ is abelian.

marginNotes.text:
As you do not know the path of the 
wind, or how the body is formed in a mother's womb, so you cannot 
understand the work of God, the Maker of all things.
